attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic at 18 weeks gestation with a three-week history of an itchy rash. This is her first pregnancy. She has a history of childhood eczema and seasonal allergies but no other medical conditions. She does not take any regular medications apart from pregnancy vitamins, and has not started any new lotions or using different chemicals. Her husband is asymptomatic.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are erythematous patches with excoriations distributed over her arms, legs and trunk, sparing the umbilical region. The rash is particularly prominent in the flexural areas. There is no change in pigmentation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features typical of atopic eruption of pregnancy (AEP which includes pruiritic folliculitis of pregnancy papular dermatoses of pregnancy or prurigo of pregnancy), including early onset in pregnancy, intense pruritus, and eczematous changes in typical atopic distribution. The presence of a personal history of atopy (childhood eczema and allergies) strongly supports this diagnosis. It may present as pruritic papules and nodules or rough erythematous patches as in this case.

Atopic eruption of pregnancy is the most common pregnancy-specific dermatosis, typically presenting in the first or second trimester. It represents a flare of atopic dermatitis or the first presentation of atopic-like changes during pregnancy. The condition is benign and does not pose risks to the fetus though the baby may also be atopic. Management is similar to eczema with ample use of moisturisers, topical steroids and avoiding soap irritants.

Pemphigoid gestationis typically presents later in pregnancy (second or third trimester) with intensely pruritic urticarial plaques that progress to tense vesicles and bullae. It characteristically begins around the umbilicus, unlike this case where the umbilical region is spared.

Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy usually occurs in the third trimester or immediate postpartum period. It typically begins within striae distensae on the abdomen and spreads to extremities, with characteristic urticarial papules and plaques rather than eczematous changes.

Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy presents with intense pruritus without primary skin lesions, though secondary excoriations may be present. It typically begins on the palms and soles and is associated with elevated bile acids and liver function tests.

Pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy is another name for polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, characterised by intensely pruritic urticarial papules and plaques that typically start within striae distensae in the third trimester.
learningPoints: Atopic eruption of pregnancy typically presents in the first or second trimester with pruritic eczematous changes.
A personal or family history of atopy is present in most cases of atopic eruption of pregnancy.
Treatment focuses on symptom control with emollients and topical corticosteroids.
references: Atopic Eruption of Pregnancy. The Australasian College of Dermatologists. 2023.  Available at: https://www.dermcoll.edu.au/atoz/atopic-eruption-of-pregnancy/
Sachdeva S. The dermatoses of pregnancy. Indian Journal of Dermatology. 2008: 53 (3).  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2763729/
